findings.description: Dedup key is only msg.to, so two different messages (distinct subject/body) to the same recipient are treated as duplicates and the second is silently skipped, contradicting the intuitive 'duplicate message' contract in the docstring.

flush() clears _pending but never clears _seen, so any recipient that received one email is permanently blocked from being enqueued again for the queue's lifetime (and _seen grows unbounded) unless clear_dedup_cache() is called manually.

No per-message error isolation: an exception from smtplib.SMTP() or sendmail() (host down / one bad recipient) propagates out of flush() and skips the _pending.clear() on line 65, so already-delivered messages remain in _pending and are re-sent on the next flush(), causing duplicate emails and inflated stats.sent.

flush() has no try/except around the loop and no graceful degradation; a single failing message aborts the entire batch with no recovery, and despite a configured module logger no error is logged (silent failure path).

Email header / CRLF injection: _render interpolates unsanitized msg.to and msg.subject directly into CRLF-delimited headers, letting a value containing \r\n inject arbitrary headers (e.g. Bcc) or body — noted in passing, defer detail to critic-security.
A fresh SMTP connection and handshake is opened and torn down per message instead of reusing one connection for the batch — noted in passing, defer detail to critic-performance.
Skipped (deduped) enqueues and successful sends produce no log output despite a module-level logger, leaving the silent dedup-drop and send activity unobservable.

flush() maintains both a local 'sent' counter and self.stats.sent in parallel (redundant), and stats.sent is incremented per successful send so it over-counts when messages are re-sent after a partial-failure abort.
